questions.specification: A company that exclusively manufactures specialized diagnostic equipment for private hospitals decides to repackage and market its existing line of digital infrared thermometers to individual consumers for home use. According to the Ansoff Matrix, which corporate strategy is being implemented?

Northbay Kitchenware (NK) is a mid-sized UK manufacturer of cookware. NK’s mission is to “make reliable tools for everyday cooking”. In the last two years, low-cost competitors have reduced NK’s market share.

NK’s board is considering a new three-year corporate strategy. They have gathered the information below.

- NK currently sells two core product lines (saucepans and frying pans) through retailers and its website.
- Its brand is known for durability and a 10-year warranty.
- Cash reserves are limited and the board wants to avoid high risk.

Strategic options under discussion:
- **Option A — Market development:** enter two new geographic markets by partnering with an established distributor.
- **Option B — Product development:** launch a range of smart thermometers and an app that integrates with NK cookware.
- **Option C — Related diversification (acquisition):** acquire “PanPro”, a small company making induction hobs, to offer a bundle: hob + cookware.

A draft extract from NK’s internal strategy document states:
> “The chosen corporate strategy must align with our mission, be deliverable with our resources, strengthen the brand, and provide a clear basis for design teams to make consistent product and communication decisions across the portfolio.”

![A simplified corporate strategy map on one page showing: Mission at the top, then Corporate objectives, then three strategy options (A market development, B product development, C related diversification), and at the bottom 'Design implications: product features, branding, channel materials, after-sales service'.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/cca7c417-eb95-4f7b-a356-2284516b36bb.png)

Figure 1: NK’s draft corporate strategy map

![A table titled 'Option comparison (board draft)' listing three rows (A, B, C) and columns: 'Risk', 'Investment', 'Time to impact', 'Fit with brand'. Option A: medium/medium/medium/high. Option B: medium/high/slow/medium. Option C: high/high/fast/medium.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/499b17da-809f-4b19-b10b-ea967605ba12.png)

Figure 2: Board draft comparison of strategic options

parts.content: State two reasons why a corporate strategy is important for a company such as Northbay Kitchenware.
parts.markscheme:
- Provides a long-term direction and priorities for the whole business (what markets/products to compete in) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Aligns functional decisions (e.g., design, marketing, operations) with corporate objectives/mission <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps allocate scarce resources/investment across a portfolio of opportunities <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Creates a basis for evaluating trade-offs and managing risk over time <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Outline how a clear corporate strategy can improve consistency in NK’s product portfolio and brand communications.
parts.markscheme:
- Sets a coherent positioning/value proposition that guides product/brand decisions across all lines <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides common criteria/standards (e.g., quality level, features, tone of voice) so touchpoints look and feel aligned across channels <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces conflicting decisions between teams by clarifying priorities and target markets <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Identify which corporate strategy type best describes Option A, Option B and Option C.
parts.markscheme:
- Option A: market development (Ansoff) / growth by entering new markets with existing products <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Option B: product development (Ansoff) / growth by new products to existing markets <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Option C: related diversification (Ansoff) via acquisition/vertical adjacency into complementary products <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how choosing Option C could change NK’s corporate strategy scope and the decisions the design function must make across the portfolio.
parts.markscheme:
- Broadens corporate scope from cookware-only to a wider cooking-system offer (appliance + cookware), changing the value proposition <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Requires portfolio architecture decisions (bundles, tiers, compatibility standards, shared platforms) to create a coherent system <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Introduces new brand/endorsement choices (single masterbrand vs sub-brand for hobs; managing acquired brand equity) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adds new user journeys and touchpoints to design (installation, safety/compliance, onboarding, servicing) beyond current products <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increases need for cross-company integration guidelines so acquired products match NK quality, aesthetics and UX expectations <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 5 marks</note>

parts.content: Discuss how NK’s design team should translate the chosen corporate strategy into actionable guidance. Refer to at least two types of corporate strategy from the case study in your answer.
parts.markscheme:
- Derives clear design objectives from corporate objectives/mission (e.g., “durability + everyday reliability”) and turns them into measurable criteria <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Creates portfolio-level rules (product architecture, common components, quality levels, naming) to ensure coherence across current and new offers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Defines brand and communication guidelines that support the strategy (tone, claims, evidence, consistent retail/online assets) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links strategic choice to user/market requirements (e.g., for market development: localisation, standards, language, channel constraints) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- For product development: sets guidance for feature prioritisation, usability, and compatibility with existing products/brand promise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- For related diversification/acquisition: establishes integration guidance (shared design language, interface consistency, service experience, safety/compliance) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shows awareness of constraints and feasibility (resources, risk, time-to-impact) and how guidance helps teams make trade-offs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Proposes methods to implement and control guidance (design briefs, stage-gates, design reviews, KPI tracking) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Justifies recommendations with reference to the case data (risk/investment/brand fit table; limited cash; warranty reputation) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 9 marks</note>

questions.specification: A company that produces high-performance waterproof jackets for professional hikers decides to start selling the exact same product line in a new geographical territory where it has never operated before. Which corporate strategy from the Ansoff Matrix is being employed?

A furniture manufacturer implements several new initiatives as part of its Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) strategy. The company uses the Triple Bottom Line (TBL) framework to evaluate its performance.

Which row correctly matches a specific initiative to its corresponding Triple Bottom Line (TBL) pillar?

| | Initiative | TBL Pillar |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | Implementing a $$15\%$$ reduction in carbon emissions | Social |
| B | Increasing the annual dividend paid to shareholders | Environmental |
| C | Providing comprehensive health insurance for all staff | Social |
| D | Streamlining production to decrease raw material waste | Economic |
